cot_prefix: Okay, let's tackle this question. The passage is about Mr. Williams and his encounter with a policeman at a crossroads. First, the setup: the crossroads has a "SLOW" sign, so drivers should slow down but don't need to stop unless something is coming. Mr. Williams follows the sign by slowing down, looks both ways, and then drives across without stopping completely. He hears a police whistle and stops. The policeman accuses him of not stopping, but Mr. Williams points out the sign says "SLOW," not "STOP." The policeman then realizes he was in the wrong street. The question is asking what Mr. Williams was doing when he was stopped by the policeman. The options are A to D. Let's break them down: A) was driving at a high speed – But the sign said "SLOW," and Mr. Williams did slow down. The policeman didn't mention speed, just that he didn't stop. So probably not A. B) was driving onto the main road – The scenario says he was on a crossroad with a "SLOW" sign, implying he was driving onto the main road. He did slow down and then went across. So maybe B is correct here. The policeman's mistake might be about which street he's on, but the action of driving onto the main road is part of the context. C) was going to stop his car – Mr. Williams did stop after hearing the whistle, but the question is about when he was stopped. The action of stopping after being whistled at might not be what the question
